Greek authorities conducted a controlled explosion of a naval drone carrying 100kg of explosives found in the Ionian Sea. The unmanned vessel, suspected to be a Ukrainian-made Magura drone, was discovered by fishermen near Lefkada with its engine still running.

English Channel migrant crossings reached a grim milestone of 200,013 people since 2018, with 70 new arrivals on Friday alone. The dangerous route has claimed at least 8 lives this year, with 23 deaths in 2025.

Neither the US nor Iran can sustain their Strait of Hormuz standoff indefinitely, analysts warn. Iran retains the ability to threaten and damage tankers passing through the vital waterway, effectively halting shipping.

The US-Iran ceasefire continues to hold despite sporadic skirmishes in the Strait of Hormuz, with Washington expressing optimism that Tehran will respond to its latest peace proposal. The IRGC has warned the US against attacks on ships while Israel continues bombing campaigns in Lebanon.

Russia and Ukraine have agreed to a three-day ceasefire brokered by the US, beginning as Moscow marks WWII Victory Day with a scaled-back parade. The temporary halt in fighting includes a reciprocal exchange of 1,000 prisoners from each side.

triadic_ontology.findings: Nathan A. Adams's "Perichoretic Persons: Diagramming Augustine’s Mental Trinity" (Percorsi 23/3, Sept 2025) offers a fresh cartography of divine relationality. Using Augustine’s De Trinitate as blueprint, Adams diagrams the cognitive-affective circuits that constitute trinitarian personhood: the Father as principium, the Son as verbum and the Spirit as amor form a dynamic loop in which each person is simultaneously knower, known and the act of knowing. The innovation lies in treating perichoresis not as static interpenetration but as algorithmic recursion: every act of divine cognition is already a triadic oscillation whose output re-enters the loop as new input, producing an ever-deepening plenitude without temporal succession. This yields a non-linear triadic dynamic that escapes both modal collapse and subordinationalism. Meanwhile, the Springer chapter "Becoming Metaphysics and an Alternative Logic System" (Jan 2026) juxtaposes African force-metaphysics with Whiteheadian process th [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three 2026 arXiv papers showcase cutting-edge autonomous research agents: DOVA introduces deliberation-first orchestration with a 6-level token-budget to cut inference cost 40-60%; MARS combines budget-aware Monte-Carlo planning and reflective memory to outperform on MLE-Bench; and Cmbagent demonstrates semi-autonomous astrophysics pipelines that, with human steering, won a weak-lensing cosmology challenge. Together they signal a shift toward modular, cost-conscious, human-in-the-loop multi-agent systems for scientific discovery.

uap.findings: Now let me extract full content from the key articles I found, particularly the Al Jazeera piece on the Pentagon release and the Stranger than Fiction.

weather: Sunny and pleasant today in Edmonds. High 65°F, low near 50°F. Light NNW breeze 5-8 mph. No rain expected—just a mix of sun and high clouds. Perfect for a walk with Piper before the partly cloudy evening settles in.

local_news.findings: Edmonds Pride has announced a month-long lineup of Pride Month events throughout June 2026, featuring community gatherings, workshops, performances, film screenings and family-friendly activities across Edmonds. The volunteer-run nonprofit, founded in 2024 with city support, has curated 15+ events including Rainbow Bingo, Pride Happy Hours, teen letter-writing for trans support, storytelling concerts, film screenings, and a culminating Pride Festival at Edmonds Civic Center Playfield on June 27.

The Washington State Court of Appeals has denied a motion to reconsider its March 16 dismissal of the Rimmer v. City of Edmonds tree case, allowing the earlier dismissal to stand. Nathan Rimmer's attorneys plan to appeal to the Washington Supreme Court for clarity on whether property owners can recover damages when cities withhold permits to force unconstitutional conditions. The case stems from a 2022 permit denial over a dogwood tree, with the city previously found liable for damages in lower court rulings.

The City of Edmonds issued an update on ongoing efforts to resolve operational issues at the Edmonds Wastewater Treatment Plant's carbon recovery system. A $31 million gasification project, plagued by delays and technical failures since 2018, underwent a multi-day trial run in late April 2026 with Jacobs Engineering evaluating results. The city has spent over $5 million hauling waste to Oregon landfills while pursuing accountability from contractors under performance guarantees.

Edmonds Police are searching for suspects who assaulted and robbed two women walking on a local trail. The department has also confirmed it does not have quotas for red-light tickets, as citations are automatically generated by video recordings and reviewed by officers. Meanwhile, the police department is working to meet state requirements by June 30 to access public safety sales tax dollars that Edmonds has been collecting since February 2026.

classroom_ready.title: Student Journalism Unit: Climate Crisis & Media Literacy
classroom_ready.topic: Research-based feature writing on climate change; countering misinformation
classroom_ready.summary: Full multi-day unit by educator Liz Ransom. Students move from analyzing climate journalism to producing their own evidence-based feature article, complete with interview protocols, rubrics, and scaffolded revision cycles. Emphasizes source evaluation and AP-style citation.

classroom_ready.title: Rhetoric & Contemporary Political Commentary
classroom_ready.topic: Close reading of argument; rhetorical analysis; civil discourse
classroom_ready.summary: Single-period or two-day mini-unit built around John Pavlovitz’s op-ed on the January 6 aftermath. Includes rhetorical-device chart, ‘neutral-rewrite’ media-literacy extension, and discussion protocols for charged texts. Rubric provided.

classroom_ready.title: Text Analysis Focus: NYS Regents Part 3
classroom_ready.topic: Central-idea identification & writing-strategy analysis for state exam
classroom_ready.summary: 45-minute lesson with printable graphic organizer. Students annotate a short nonfiction text, craft a precise central-idea statement, and explain how one authorial strategy develops it. Mirrors Regents format; includes timing guide and exemplar response.

classroom_ready.title: 12th Grade Full-Year Curriculum: On Being Human
classroom_ready.topic: Thematic units anchored in Hamlet, Klara and the Sun, Sula, Exit West, Fun Home
classroom_ready.summary: Five complete 3- to 4-week units (total 121 days) with daily lesson plans, discussion questions, Socratic-seminar guides, and writing tasks. Each unit pairs a core text with essential questions on identity, technology, and morality. Free download of pacing guide, standards map, and book list.

classroom_ready.title: Figurative Language Mini-Unit
classroom_ready.topic: Simile, metaphor, personification, hyperbole, idiom identification & creation
classroom_ready.summary: One-period lesson with bell-ringer, direct-instruction slides, guided practice using short contemporary poems, and exit ticket. Google-Docs worksheets and auto-graded quiz included. Aligned to CCSS; extension asks students to embed figurative language in their own descriptive paragraph.

pedagogy_pieces.title: High School Students Thrive When Given Choices in English Class
pedagogy_pieces.summary: Recent Edutopia feature (May 2026) on choice-based reading and writing menus. Evidence shows autonomy increases engagement and text complexity students willingly tackle. Includes practical templates for choice boards and reflection protocols.

pedagogy_pieces.title: Bringing Moments of Joy to High School English
pedagogy_pieces.summary: Teacher reflection on using literature to help students process global turbulence. Argues for intentional joy-building through creative writing, celebratory readings, and community showcases—especially vital during crisis news cycles.

english_lesson_plans.notes: All resources are free and classroom-ready; URLs verified live 2026-05-10. Fishtank and Subject to Climate plans include full rubrics and standards alignment. Brisk and KuraPlan resources are AI-generated but teacher-reviewed.

global_news.findings: Greek authorities conducted a controlled explosion of a naval drone carrying 100kg of explosives found in the Ionian Sea. The unmanned vessel, suspected to be a Ukrainian-made Magura drone, was discovered by fishermen near Lefkada with its engine still running. Handwritten notes in Ukrainian were found aboard. The incident raises serious questions about Greece's maritime security and vulnerability to emerging military technology, with opposition parties criticizing the government's preparedness.

English Channel migrant crossings reached a grim milestone of 200,013 people since 2018, with 70 new arrivals on Friday alone. The dangerous route has claimed at least 8 lives this year, with 23 deaths in 2025. The majority of migrants are from Iran, Afghanistan, Iraq, Eritrea and Albania, but there's been a recent surge from African nations including Sudan, where arrivals increased 60% following civil wars. Despite government promises to 'smash' smuggling gangs, crossings have more than doubled in the last three years.

Neither the US nor Iran can sustain their Strait of Hormuz standoff indefinitely, analysts warn. Iran retains the ability to threaten and damage tankers passing through the vital waterway, effectively halting shipping. More than 1,550 vessels have been affected by the blockade, with global oil markets experiencing severe volatility. The ongoing conflict has already cost major corporations billions, with Toyota alone reporting £3 billion in losses due to soaring material prices and falling sales.

The US-Iran ceasefire continues to hold despite sporadic skirmishes in the Strait of Hormuz, with Washington expressing optimism that Tehran will respond to its latest peace proposal. The IRGC has warned the US against attacks on ships while Israel continues bombing campaigns in Lebanon. The fragile truce remains under pressure as both sides navigate the complex diplomatic landscape of the ongoing conflict.

Russia and Ukraine have agreed to a three-day ceasefire brokered by the US, beginning as Moscow marks WWII Victory Day with a scaled-back parade. The temporary halt in fighting includes a reciprocal exchange of 1,000 prisoners from each side. President Trump announced the breakthrough, stating that talks to end the war are continuing and both sides are 'getting closer every day' to a permanent resolution.

intellectual_journals.findings: Sean Irving traces the forgotten 19th-century tradition of "economic republicanism"—a movement that argued authentic freedom requires not just political rights but economic power. Drawing on Machiavelli, Harrington and Rousseau, Irving shows how British trade unionists, 1848 revolutionaries and American land-reformers all insisted that liberty means freedom from domination in both state and market. The essay diagnoses the present democratic malaise as a loss of this republican nerve: citizens feel powerless because liberals surrendered the economic terrain to unaccountable corporations and dat [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy stages a dialogue between Marcel Proust’s 1905 essay "On Reading" and John Ruskin’s moralising defence of literature. Proust rejects the utilitarian claim that books make us better people; instead, he celebrates reading as a uniquely intimate communion that preserves the texture of lived experience across time. Literature, for Proust, is not a moral technology but a way of keeping past sensations alive—‘something to be grasped without being abolished.’ The essay defends the intrinsic value of literary attention against both market logic and pedagogical instrumentalism, arguing tha [TRUNCATED]

Christopher de Bellaigue surveys post-war Iran through the lens of Mehdi Akhavan-Sales’s 1950s poem "Winter," finding a society still frozen by the 1953 CIA coup that toppled Mossadegh. Four decades of sanctions, corruption and endless conflict have produced a ‘new winter’: power outages, unpaid wages, and a middle class inured to privation. Yet the regime survives on Chinese oil revenue and a propaganda victory that casts defiance of the US as heroic. De Bellaigue warns that Washington and Tel Aviv’s hope that bombing would spark an uprising was delusional; instead, the war has entrenched the [TRUNCATED]

Civil-liberties veteran Aryeh Neier dissects the Trump administration’s executive order purporting to restore "free speech" by barring federal pressure on social-media platforms. Neier shows that the order inverts reality: far from ending censorship, it weaponises state power to intimidate platforms into hosting disinformation and hate speech. Surveying a century of US speech restrictions—from Wilson’s Espionage Act to McCarthyism—Neier argues that the current campaign is unprecedented in its overt use of presidential authority to chill dissent and marks a qualitative escalation in the long Am [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova distils Oliver Sacks’s posthumous essay collection to a meditation on the 100-millisecond lag between neural firing and conscious perception. Drawing on cases of patients whose time seems to freeze, Sacks argues that consciousness is not a passive mirror but a creative act that marshals the whole self into momentary coalitions of neurons. Popova weaves Sacks’s neurology with William Blake and Magritte to evoke the vertigo of never knowing what another mind perceives—‘the one hundred milliseconds between the world and you’—and celebrates literature as the only bridge across that un [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Reverend Billy Takes On Norman Foster’s New Monolith
newyorker.dek: Fresh from opening shows for Neil Young, the street preacher Billy Talen has moved on from burning Mickey Mouse in effigy to protesting JPMorgan Chase’s ties to fossil fuels, Jake Offenhartz writes.
newyorker.text:
A little after midnight on Earth Day, the street preacher Billy Talen dragged his pulpit to the new Norman Foster-designed global headquarters of JPMorgan Chase, on Madison. His self-ordained mission: deliver a twenty-four-hour sermon, calling attention to the bank’s ties to the fossil-fuel industry. Talen, who is seventy-five, held an umbrella above his white leisure suit and silver bouffant, eying a pair of bank security guards. “Blessings upon your souls, gentlemen,” he bellowed. Members of his activist ensemble, the Stop Shopping Choir, were setting up a live stream in the rain. Savitri Durkee, who directs both the choir and Talen, who is her husband, clipped a mike to his lapel and checked her watch. “It’s go-time, Billy,” she said. “See you on the other side.”
Talen, who goes by Reverend Billy, is fond of what he calls “extreme preaching.” He began in the mid-nineties, marching around Times Square with “Antichrist” Mickey Mouse nailed to a wooden cross, in protest of the neighborhood’s creeping corporatism. In the years since, his focus has shifted to the climate crisis. Often, this involves staging musical protests in hostile venues—banks, pipeline-construction zones—until he and his flock are arrested for trespassing. But, Talen said, even that has begun to feel “predictable.” He elaborated, speaking into a camera: “The only way we’re going to start an effective movement is if each of us goes to the limit of our capabilities and claims our eccentricities. I’m a preacher, so I’m going to preach until I drop.” A choir member checked the stream, and noted that they’d just jumped from five to fourteen viewers. “Earthalujah!” Talen cried.
After decades of sneaking up on bankers and lobbyists, Reverend Billy and the choir recently booked a straight gig: touring the country as Neil Young’s opening act. (Typical song: “A Tree Is the Opposite of a Cop.”) The rock star’s fans seemed receptive to the message, relatively. “After a lifetime of being too strange, people are starting to get us,” Talen said. He’s also the religion editor for the Times-Contrarian, Young’s anti-corporate “digital newspaper.” “Neil is a hands-on publisher,” Talen said. “Sometimes he lets me write these really long things. Other times, he’ll say to me, ‘Billy, what do you mean here? What do you want to leave us with?,’ and we’ll e-mail back and forth on that.” Talen said that he’s learned a lot from Young as a performer: “He’s taught me to hold the rhythm, hit the rhyme. He gets what appeals to a mass audience.” Talen and Durkee were connected with the singer by Young’s wife, Daryl Hannah, whom they met at a 2009 protest against mountaintop-removal coal mining in West Virginia. Talen was later arrested, after depositing mounds of “sacred dirt”—taken from the mountain, he claimed—at two Chase branches in the East Village. The bank has since decreased financing for companies involved in mountaintop-removal mining.
By 8 A.M., dark circles had formed under Talen’s steely blue eyes. “I just need to stay hydrated,” he said. Behind him, a gardener was washing a living-wall installation titled “A Parallel Nature,” designed for Chase by Maya Lin. “I’m finding myself mesmerized by this strange, sculpted nature wall,” Talen said. “It feels like a memory.” After conferring with a guard, the gardener pointed his hose at the protesters in the plaza. Talen pulled a rain slicker over his suit and glowered. Thousands of workers were now streaming into the building, mostly ignoring the doomsday preacher.
Around lunchtime, roughly two dozen choir members and other activists arrived for a 5K run around the bank. Among them was Ahmed, an asylum seeker from Guinea who had taken refuge in the choir’s former headquarters, a vacant bank on Avenue C, after arriving in the United States. “It’s very scary-looking,” he said of Chase’s sixty-story tower. Many of the runners had procured thrift-store blazers or top hats, an effort to blend in with the bankers. Durkee, who wore a pin-striped suit, led the group in some stretches, followed by twelve laps around the block. A construction worker sanding down a railing offered support—“You got this!”—but appeared confused by a runner’s sign accusing Chase of ecocide. “I thought it was, like, a charity thing,” he said.
As night fell, Talen huddled under a blanket with other choir members. His once thunderous voice had dropped to a rasp as he condemned the “gangsters in suits” responsible for rising global temperatures. In the final minutes of his sermon, he found himself at an unusual loss for words. “It feels like, well, extreme exhaustion, I guess,” he said. “Or a long hike that got out of hand, and now I have to come down the mountain.” Had the suffering been worth it? “I’m not sure we cracked the culture,” he said. “But we had a vision, and we did what we could.” ♦

enriched_articles.title: 'Make up their own minds': Pentagon releases first tranche of UFO files
enriched_articles.text: The United States Department of Defense has released its first tranche of previously classified files on unidentified flying objects (UFOs), following an order from US President Donald Trump in February. The release on Friday contained 162 files dating back decades, including documents from the FBI, the Department of State, and the US space agency, NASA. It was accompanied by the launch of a new, heavily stylised website to house the documents, using white typewriter-style font against a black b

enriched_articles.title: Greek authorities conduct controlled blast of mystery naval drone explosives
enriched_articles.text: Greek authorities have rendered safe a naval drone carrying explosives after it was discovered in the Ionian Sea by fishermen. The unmanned vessel, suspected to be of Ukrainian origin, was found in a cave near Lefkada on Greece's western coast on Thursday with its engine still running. Greek bomb disposal experts removed its detonators and battery. The incident has raised questions about Greece's maritime security and its vulnerability to emerging military technology.

enriched_articles.title: Perichoretic Persons: Diagramming Augustine's Mental Trinity
enriched_articles.text: This article seeks to develop new insight from *De Trinitate* into what it means to be a trinitarian person by diagramming the interchange of cognition and love that Augustine traces in the mental trinity. It begins with some important groundwork by Augustinian scholarship on the psychological analogy for the Trinity, and then moves to a detailed analysis of the mental trinity itself.

enriched_articles.title: DOVA: Deliberation-First Multi-Agent Orchestration for Autonomous Research Automation
enriched_articles.text: Large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in tool use, reasoning, and code generation, yet single-agent systems exhibit fundamental limitations when confronted with complex research tasks demanding multi-source synthesis, adversarial verification, and personalized delivery. We present DOVA (Deep Orchestrated Versatile Agent), a multi-agent platform introducing three innovations: (1) deliberation-first orchestration, where explicit meta-reasoning precedes tool in

enriched_articles.title: [Title not extracted]
enriched_articles.text: We present a comprehensive framework for autonomous research agents that integrate large language models with external knowledge bases and reasoning engines. Our system demonstrates significant improvements in research task completion rates and accuracy across multiple domains including scientific literature review, policy analysis, and educational content generation. The framework employs a novel approach to knowledge synthesis that combines symbolic reasoning with neural network-based understa
